title: Agent-to-agent payment authorization circuit
description: Add a PaymentAuthorization circuit that extends the delegation chain to include spend limits. The delegatee's scopeCommitment would bind a maxSpendWei field alongside the permission bitmask, enabling agents to transact up to a cryptographically enforced budget without returning to the human for approval. This is the missing primitive for autonomous agent commerce — without it, every agent transaction requires human-in-the-loop, which breaks at scale. Wire format: extend Delegation public outputs with a spendCommitment = Poseidon3(maxSpendWei, cumulativeSpentWei, delegateeCredCommitment).

title: Lightweight session token for high-frequency interactions
description: The current handshake requires a full Groth16 + PLONK proof per interaction, which is too expensive for agentic commerce where agents may exchange hundreds of messages per minute. Add a SessionToken circuit that proves 'I completed a valid handshake within the last N blocks' by checking a session commitment against an on-chain session registry. One heavy handshake unlocks a lightweight session proof (~5k constraints) valid for a configurable TTL. This is the difference between a demo and a product — no commerce protocol survives without session semantics.

title: Vercel AI SDK + OpenAI function-calling adapter
description: The seed candidates target LangChain and CrewAI, but the fastest-growing agent deployment surface is Vercel AI SDK and OpenAI's function-calling API. Build a @bolyra/ai adapter that exposes proveHandshake() and verifyHandshake() as OpenAI-compatible tool definitions, plus a Vercel AI SDK middleware that automatically attaches Bolyra proofs to tool calls marked as sensitive. This meets developers where they already are — not where we wish they were. Ship as an npm package with a Next.js example app.

title: On-chain agent capability registry for discovery
description: Agentic commerce requires agents to discover and evaluate each other before handshaking. Add an AgentRegistry contract where operators publish capability descriptors (model family, permission bitmask, service category) as structured on-chain metadata linked to their credentialCommitment. Agents can query the registry, filter by required scope bits, and initiate handshakes only with compatible counterparts. Without discovery, Bolyra is authentication for agents that already know each other — which is a much smaller market.

title: Privacy-preserving delegation audit trail
description: Enterprise and credit union adoption requires auditability. Emit structured events from the IdentityRegistry that encode the delegation chain topology (hop count, scope narrowing ratio, timestamp deltas) without revealing the identity of any participant. Add a DelegationAuditLog contract that indexes these events into an append-only on-chain log queryable by session nonce. Compliance officers can verify 'this agent acted within delegated authority' without learning who the human or agent is. This is the feature that gets you past procurement at regulated institutions.

title: EIP-1186 storage proof cross-chain root sync
description: Implement cross-chain root synchronization between Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon using EIP-1186 storage proofs. The IdentityRegistry on the source chain (Base) stores humanTree and agentTree roots in known storage slots. A relay contract on each target chain accepts an RLP-encoded storage proof against a recent L1 block hash, verifies it on-chain via MPT verification, and updates a local rootCache mapping. This eliminates trust assumptions from oracle-based bridges and lets proofs generated against Base roots be verified on Arbitrum/Polygon with ~1 epoch latency.

title: Chain-agnostic nullifier registry
description: The current nullifier check (usedNonces, usedNullifiers) is per-contract, meaning a proof verified on Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if both chains accept the same roots. Add a chainId binding to the nullifier derivation: nullifier = Poseidon3(scope, secret, chainId) for humans and Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chainId) for agents. Update HumanUniqueness.circom and AgentPolicy.circom to take chainId as a public input. This is a critical correctness fix before any multi-chain deployment.

title: Hardhat multi-chain deployment and test scaffold
description: Create a Hardhat deployment script and integration test suite that deploys IdentityRegistry + all three verifiers to Base Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Polygon Amoy in one command. Include a cross-chain smoke test: enroll a human on Base, relay the root to Arbitrum via the storage proof contract, and verify a handshake on Arbitrum against the relayed root. Outputs a deployment manifest JSON with contract addresses per chain. This is the minimum scaffold needed for any multi-chain work.

title: Batched root relay with gas amortization
description: Storage proofs are expensive to verify on-chain (~200k gas per MPT proof on L2). Build a batched relay that accumulates root updates into a Merkle tree of roots, posts one aggregate proof per epoch (e.g., every 100 blocks), and lets target chains verify inclusion of any specific root against the aggregate. This reduces per-root relay cost by 10-50x depending on batch size. Implement as a RootRelayBatcher.sol contract with a keeper script that monitors source chain root changes and submits batches.

title: Cross-chain conformance test vectors
description: Extend the conformance test suite with 15+ JSON test vectors specific to multi-chain scenarios: valid cross-chain root relay, stale relayed root rejection, nullifier replay across chains (must fail), chainId mismatch in proof (must fail), root relay with invalid storage proof, delegation chain that spans two chains via relayed roots, and epoch boundary edge cases. These vectors should be chain-agnostic (parameterized by chainId) so any new chain integration can run them as a compliance gate.

title: W3C DID method specification for did:bolyra
description: Define a complete W3C DID method binding: did:bolyra:<identityCommitment>. Specify the DID Document structure (verification methods map to EdDSA Baby Jubjub keys, service endpoints for handshake initiation), the resolution algorithm (on-chain lookup via IdentityRegistry humanTree/agentTree), and CRUD operations (create = enroll, deactivate = revoke). Include a Verifiable Credential issuance flow where successful handshake verification produces a VC with the nullifier as proof, enabling interop with any W3C VC-compatible wallet.

title: Conformance test vectors with multi-chain coverage
description: Produce 60+ JSON test vectors organized by circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) covering: valid proofs, expired credentials, revoked identities, stale roots (both human and agent), scope subset violations, cumulative bit encoding violations (bit 4 without bits 2+3), delegation chain at depths 1/2/3/4 (4 must fail), phantom delegatee rejection, nonce replay, and cross-chain root sync scenarios. Each vector includes input signals, expected public outputs, and expected pass/fail status. Ship as a standalone JSON file with a JSON Schema so other implementations can validate their parser.

title: Interoperability profile for multi-verifier deployments
description: Write an interop profile document (modeled on OIDC Conformance) that specifies the exact wire format for proof exchange between provers and verifiers across different deployments. Define: (1) a JSON-based proof envelope with fields for circuit type, public signals array, proof bytes, and metadata; (2) content-type negotiation (application/bolyra-proof+json); (3) version negotiation via a 'protocol-version' header; (4) mandatory-to-implement cipher suites (BN254/Groth16 for human, BN254/PLONK for agent). This enables independent verifier implementations to accept proofs from any compliant prover.

title: NIST SP 800-63B authenticator assurance level mapping
description: Produce a formal analysis mapping Bolyra's authentication factors to NIST AAL levels. HumanUniqueness (EdDSA secret + Merkle membership) maps to a single-factor cryptographic authenticator; when combined with AgentPolicy (operator EdDSA + model attestation), the handshake constitutes multi-factor at AAL2. Document the gap analysis for AAL3 (requires hardware-bound key — currently not enforced), and specify what the platform-attestation-circuit extension would need to close that gap. Deliverable: a 3-page analysis with a compliance matrix table suitable for credit union security reviews.

title: ABNF grammar for proof payloads and enrollment messages
description: Define a machine-parseable ABNF grammar (RFC 5234) for all protocol messages: enrollment requests (human and agent), handshake proof bundles (human proof + agent proof + optional delegation chain), delegation tokens, and revocation notices. Each message type gets a canonical binary encoding (for on-chain calldata efficiency) and a JSON encoding (for SDK ergonomics). Include a CDDL (RFC 8610) companion spec for CBOR encoding, targeting constrained IoT verifiers. This removes ambiguity from the IETF draft's wire format section and enables automated parser generation.

title: Migrate HumanUniqueness from Groth16 to PLONK
description: HumanUniqueness currently uses Groth16 requiring a trusted ceremony (reusing Semaphore v4's depth-20 ceremony). Migrating to PLONK (matching AgentPolicy and Delegation) eliminates ceremony trust assumptions, unifies the proving stack to a single backend, and simplifies the IdentityRegistry contract by removing the separate IGroth16Verifier interface. The circuit is small (~15k constraints for BabyPbk + Poseidon + Merkle), well within PLONK's efficiency range. Deliverable: recompiled HumanUniqueness circuit with PLONK proving key, updated HumanVerifier.sol, and unified verifier interface in IdentityRegistry.

title: Add blinding salt to scope commitment in AgentPolicy and Delegation
description: scopeCommitment = Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment) is vulnerable to offline brute-force since the 64-bit bitmask has at most 2^64 preimages and credentialCommitment is a public Merkle leaf. Adding a random blinding salt as a third Poseidon input (Poseidon3) makes the commitment computationally hiding. Cost is ~1 additional Poseidon round (~300 constraints). Deliverable: updated AgentPolicy.circom and Delegation.circom with blindingSalt private input, updated scope commitment computation, and matching contract-side changes to accept the new public signal layout.

title: Replace EdDSA verification with Poseidon-based commitment binding in AgentPolicy
description: EdDSA verification in AgentPolicy costs ~30k constraints (60%+ of the circuit budget). The operator signature proves the operator authorized the credential, but this can be achieved more cheaply: the operator signs off-chain (verified at enrollment time on-chain), and the circuit only needs to prove knowledge of the preimage to the credential commitment that is already in the Merkle tree. Remove the in-circuit EdDSA verify, save ~30k constraints, cutting proving time roughly in half. The security model shifts to: Merkle inclusion proves the credential was operator-approved at enrollment. Deliverable: simplified AgentPolicy.circom (~20k constraints), updated test vectors, benchmarks showing proving time reduction.

title: Extract shared Merkle + range-check template across all three circuits
description: All three circuits independently implement Merkle inclusion with BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH) and range checks via Num2Bits(64). Extract a reusable BolyraCredentialBase template that encapsulates: range-checked uint64 inputs, Merkle membership proof, and nullifier derivation. This reduces code duplication across AgentPolicy, Delegation, and HumanUniqueness, makes auditing easier, and provides a clean foundation for new circuits (e.g., platform attestation). Deliverable: circuits/lib/BolyraBase.circom template, refactored circuits importing it, no constraint count change, all existing tests passing.

title: Fix under-constrained expiry comparison in Delegation circuit
description: The Delegation circuit takes delegatorExpiry and delegateeExpiry as private inputs and must enforce delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry (scope narrowing includes temporal narrowing). The current circuit declares these signals and range-checks them but the truncated source suggests the LessEqThan comparator may be missing or incomplete. Without this constraint, a delegatee could set an arbitrarily long expiry exceeding the delegator's, breaking the temporal narrowing invariant. Deliverable: verified LessEqThan(64) constraint between delegateeExpiry and delegatorExpiry, a test vector proving rejection of expiry-widening attempts, and a second constraint ensuring delegateeExpiry > currentTimestamp (liveness).

title: Prove delegation expiry narrowing is fully constrained
description: The Delegation circuit accepts delegatorExpiry and delegateeExpiry as private inputs and claims to enforce delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry, but the constraint section is truncated in the current source. Write a Certora-style property spec (as a circom assertion test harness) that: (1) generates witness pairs where delegateeExpiry > delegatorExpiry and asserts the circuit rejects them, (2) checks that the LessThan comparator input width matches the Num2Bits(64) range check so no field-wrapping bypass exists. Deliver: a test harness with 10+ edge-case vectors (boundary, zero, max-uint64, field-near-prime) and a written invariant document.

title: Prove nullifier uniqueness across all three circuits
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy computes it differently (truncated), and Delegation has its own delegationNullifier. A formal property must prove: (1) no two distinct identity/credential pairs can produce the same nullifier within a circuit, (2) nullifiers from different circuits cannot collide (domain separation). Write a symbolic analysis showing Poseidon's collision resistance assumption suffices, and build a fuzzer that samples 10k random witness pairs per circuit confirming no empirical collisions. Deliver: written proof sketch + executable fuzzer + CI integration script.

title: Verify cumulative bit encoding invariant is enforced in-circuit
description: The Delegation circuit documents a cumulative bit encoding rule (bit 4 implies bits 2+3, bit 3 implies bit 2) but circuit enforcement must be verified. Write a formal property that exhaustively checks all 2^6 relevant bit combinations (bits 0-5) against the invariant, confirming the circuit rejects any bitmask violating cumulative encoding. Also verify AgentPolicy's scopeCommitment output correctly binds permissionBitmask to credentialCommitment so a delegatee cannot claim wider scope than granted. Deliver: exhaustive truth table test, circom assertion template, and a counterexample generator.

title: Formally bound root history race window
description: IdentityRegistry.sol uses a 30-slot circular buffer for both humanRootHistory and agentRootHistory. Prove that: (1) any root older than 30 enrollments is guaranteed to be evicted and rejected, (2) the agentRootExists/humanRootExists mappings stay exactly in sync with the buffer (no ghost entries after wraparound), (3) concurrent enrollment + verification cannot observe an inconsistent state within a single transaction. Write Solidity invariant tests (Foundry-style) plus a state machine model showing the buffer transitions. Deliver: Foundry fuzz test suite + state transition diagram + written invariant proofs.

title: Audit range check completeness across all circuits
description: AgentPolicy range-checks permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, and currentTimestamp via Num2Bits(64), but HumanUniqueness has no range check on scope or sessionNonce — if scope exceeds the field prime, the Poseidon nullifier could alias. Systematically audit every private and public input across all three circuits for missing range checks. For each input, determine if it participates in a comparison or is used in contract-side uint256 arithmetic, and flag any that lack Num2Bits constraints. Deliver: audit spreadsheet mapping every signal to its range constraint (or lack thereof), patch circom templates for missing checks, and regression test vectors.

title: Python SDK with snarkjs subprocess bridge
description: Build a PyPI package `bolyra` that wraps snarkjs via subprocess for proof generation and verification. Expose the same core API surface as @bolyra/sdk (create_identity, enroll_human, enroll_agent, prove_handshake, verify_handshake, delegate) with Pythonic conventions (snake_case, dataclasses for structured types, async/await support). Target LangChain and CrewAI developers who need mutual auth in agent pipelines. Include a `bolyra.testing` module with fixture factories for mock identities and pre-computed proofs so integration tests don't require a live snarkjs process.

title: Structured error hierarchy with recovery hints
description: Replace opaque snarkjs failures and generic Solidity reverts with a typed error hierarchy in both SDKs. Each error class (StaleRootError, RevokedIdentityError, ScopeNarrowingViolation, ExpiredCredentialError, NonceReplayError) should carry the specific failing field, the expected vs actual value, and a human-readable recovery hint (e.g., 'Root 0xabc… is stale. Call registry.refreshRoot() or re-fetch the latest Merkle proof before retrying.'). Map on-chain custom errors to SDK exceptions so developers never see raw hex revert data.

title: LangChain BolyraAuthTool for agent mutual auth
description: Build a LangChain BaseTool subclass that wraps the Python SDK to perform mutual authentication before sensitive operations. The tool accepts a session description, generates a handshake proof, verifies the counterparty's proof, and returns a structured ToolMessage with authentication status, scope, and expiry. Include a BolyraGuard callback handler that automatically triggers handshake verification when an agent chain enters a scope-gated step, so developers wire up auth with a single `callbacks=[BolyraGuard(identity)]` argument.

title: Scaffolded quickstart templates with `npx create-bolyra`
description: Create a CLI scaffolder (`npx create-bolyra`) that generates working starter projects for three personas: (1) 'human-agent handshake' — minimal 8-line script showing enrollment and mutual auth, (2) 'delegation chain' — Express middleware that verifies delegated agent credentials before forwarding requests, (3) 'langchain-guard' — Python project with a LangChain agent protected by BolyraGuard. Each template includes a local Hardhat node with pre-deployed contracts, pre-enrolled test identities, and a single `npm start` or `python main.py` command to run the full flow. Target: under 60 seconds from `npx create-bolyra` to a verified handshake.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder API
description: The current delegation flow requires manually constructing Merkle proofs, scope bitmasks, and chaining previousScopeCommitment across hops — too much ceremony for SDK users. Add a DelegationChainBuilder with a fluent API: `chain = DelegationChain.from(agentIdentity).narrow(scope=['read','write']).expireBefore(parentExpiry).delegateTo(delegateeCommitment)`. The builder internally resolves Merkle proofs from the registry, enforces cumulative bit encoding invariants (bit 4 implies 2+3), validates scope narrowing at build time with clear errors, and outputs the full proof array ready for on-chain submission. This eliminates the most common integration mistake: malformed delegation chains.

title: Python SDK with snarkjs subprocess bridge
description: Ship a `bolyra` PyPI package that wraps snarkjs via subprocess for proof generation and verification. Expose `create_identity()`, `enroll_agent()`, `prove_handshake()`, `verify_handshake()`, and `delegate()` with full type hints (pydantic models for proof artifacts, public signals, and credential structs). LangChain/CrewAI developers live in Python — without this, every integration requires hand-rolling subprocess calls to snarkjs and manually parsing JSON public signals. Include a `BolyraCredential` dataclass that serializes to/from the wire format so agent frameworks can pass credentials through tool chains without custom serialization.

title: LangChain BolyraAuthTool with structured output
description: Build a `BolyraAuthTool(BaseTool)` that performs mutual human-agent authentication before sensitive operations. The tool takes a session nonce and scope, runs `prove_handshake()` from the Python SDK, and returns a `BolyraAuthResult` pydantic model with `verified: bool`, `nullifier_hash: str`, `scope_commitment: str`, and `expiry: int`. This lets any LangChain agent chain gate downstream tools behind ZK auth — e.g., a financial agent proves it holds the `financial_read` scope bit before calling a bank API tool. Include a `BolyraAuthGuard` callback handler that automatically rejects tool calls when the agent's credential is expired or scope is insufficient.

title: CrewAI task delegation mapped to Bolyra delegation circuit
description: Create a `BolyraDelegationProcess` that hooks into CrewAI's `Task.delegate()` flow. When a CrewAI agent delegates a task to another agent, the process automatically generates a Delegation circuit proof with scope narrowing — the delegatee agent receives only the permission bits required for the subtask. The 3-hop max in IdentityRegistry maps naturally to CrewAI's manager→worker→sub-worker pattern. Store the delegation chain's `scopeCommitment` outputs in the CrewAI task context so downstream agents can present them to verifiers. This gives CrewAI users cryptographically enforced least-privilege delegation with zero custom circuit code.

title: AutoGen conversation handshake middleware
description: Build an AutoGen `BolyraHandshakeMiddleware` that injects mutual authentication into the first message of any agent-to-agent conversation. Before the conversation begins, both agents exchange HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy proofs bound to a shared session nonce. The middleware attaches the verified proof artifacts as conversation metadata, so any downstream agent or tool can check `conversation.metadata['bolyra_auth']` for scope and nullifier. This is critical for AutoGen's multi-agent chat pattern where agents from different operators interact — without per-conversation auth, there's no way to enforce that a foreign agent actually holds the credentials it claims.

title: Descriptive SDK error hierarchy with framework-friendly messages
description: Replace the current generic revert strings in IdentityRegistry.sol and add a corresponding Python/TypeScript error hierarchy: `StaleRootError`, `RevokedIdentityError`, `ScopeMismatchError`, `NonceReusedError`, `DelegationDepthExceededError`. Each error includes the specific failing value (e.g., which root hash was stale, what the expected vs actual scope was). Framework users debugging agent auth failures currently get opaque revert data — structured errors let LangChain's error callbacks and CrewAI's retry logic make intelligent decisions like re-enrolling on `StaleRootError` or requesting broader delegation on `ScopeMismatchError`.

title: Scope commitment blinding salt prevents bitmask recovery
description: The scopeCommitment in AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits is Poseidon(bitmask, credCommitment). Since bitmask is only 64 bits, an adversary knowing credCommitment (public in the agent tree) can brute-force all 2^64 bitmask values — but practically, real-world permission sets are far smaller (likely <1000 distinct masks). Replace with Poseidon3(bitmask, credCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a 254-bit private input. This breaks offline enumeration while preserving the subset-proof chain linking. Artifact: patch AgentPolicy.circom step for scopeCommitment, patch Delegation.circom chain-link verification, update IdentityRegistry.sol public signal count.

title: Integrate DelegationExpiryCheck into main Delegation circuit
description: The expiry ordering fix (DelegationExpiryCheck.circom in tier2_006) exists as a standalone fragment with 18 boundary tests, but the main Delegation.circom template referenced in the seed still lacks the LessThan/LessEqThan constraints. An attacker can delegate to an agent with a longer expiry than the delegator, or use an already-expired delegation. Merge the DelegationExpiryCheck constraints (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry, currentTimestamp < delegateeExpiry) into the canonical Delegation template, add currentTimestamp as a public input, and update the DelegationVerifier.sol public signal count from 5 to 6.

title: Constrain merkleProofLength to prevent depth manipulation
description: In HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy, merkleProofLength is a private input passed to BinaryMerkleRoot's depth parameter. If the BinaryMerkleRoot template does not internally range-check depth < MAX_DEPTH, a malicious prover could supply depth=0 (proving membership against a tree containing only their leaf as root) or depth > MAX_DEPTH (field overflow). Add an explicit constraint: merkleProofLength * (merkleProofLength - 1) ... up to MAX_DEPTH via IsEqual checks, or a simple LessThan(5) ensuring depth <= 20. Artifact: add range constraint in all three circuits, add test vectors for depth=0 and depth=MAX_DEPTH+1.

title: Harden cross-chain storage proof block hash verification
description: RootRelay.sol's _getL1BlockHash uses a simplified trust model (documented as placeholder) that returns bytes32(0) on failure but does not parse actual block header RLP or validate against a real L1 oracle like the EIP-4788 beacon root contract. An attacker controlling the oracle address could inject arbitrary state roots. Replace with EIP-4788 parentBeaconBlockRoot precompile on L2s that support it, or Axiom/Relic-style historic block hash proofs. Add a block age check (reject blocks older than ~256 blocks or staler than the root history buffer window). Artifact: production-grade _getL1BlockHash using 0x000F3df6D732807Ef1319fB7B8bB8522d0Beac02, integration test with forked mainnet.

title: Constraint-level fuzzer for all three circuits
description: No automated fuzzing exists for witness generation. A constraint-level fuzzer would generate random witness inputs (valid credentials with bit-flipped fields, expired timestamps, wrong Merkle paths, out-of-range bitmasks) and assert: (1) valid witnesses satisfy all constraints, (2) single-bit mutations in critical fields cause constraint failure. This catches under-constrained signals that manual review misses — the expiry ordering bug in AgentPolicy v1.0.0 would have been caught instantly. Build on circom-witness-rs for fast witness computation, target 10k mutations/second. Artifact: Python harness using circom_tester, mutation strategies for each input class, CI integration.
